coredata.dc:title: 2D-network of boron-functionalized N-doped graphene quantum dots for electrochemical sensing of dopamine

coredata.dc:description: A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by boron-functionalized N-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) was employed for the electrochemical determination of dopamine (DA), a neurotransmitter. The B-GQDs were obtained by hydrothermal etching of GO with H2O2 and NH3 in the presence of H3BO3. By using various techniques, including transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, molecular absorption spectrometry, fluorescence, ICP-MS, and zeta potential analysis, it was demonstrated that B-GQDs are self-organized in 2D-network, being N-doped GQDs nanoparticles crosslinked by H3BO3. The GCE modification was carried out using the drop-casting method with B-GQDs in Nafion®, previously dispersed through ultrasonication. For the first time, the synthesized nanomaterial was tested in the electroanalysis of neurotransmitters. The B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE modification led to a significant increase in peak currents in the presence of DA. The electrochemical and interfacial characteristics of the sensors were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Under optimized square wave voltammetry and supporting electrolyte conditions, a linear dependence between the anodic peak current and DA concentration was observed in the range of 0.7 to 188 μmol L−1 (R2 = 0.9991). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated at 0.05 μmol L−1. Selectivity was confirmed in the presence of potential interferents found in urine, with interference response below 12 %. The sensor exhibited excellent intra-day and inter-day repeatability, with a relative standard deviation of 4.12 % and 4.66 %, respectively. Electrochemical determination of DA was conducted in artificial urine samples (Surine®), with recovery values of 90–101 %. The new analytical platform B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE demonstrated promising characteristics for application in biological and/or pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) B-GQDs, (B) B-GQDs 200 nm, (C) B-GQDs 50 nm, and (D) B-GQDs 20 nm (inset: fringe pattern). AFM images of (E) B-GQDs and (F) height profile. (G) DLS investigation (particle size distribution and normalized-intensity time autocorrelation function (inset)) of B-GQDs.
(A) Surveys and (B) C1s XPS spectra of B-GQDs and GO, (C) B1s spectra of B-GQDs, and (D) N1s spectra of B-GQDs.

(A) CV for DA 70 μmol L−1 using GCE, B-GQDs/GCE; Nafion®/GCE and B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE. Inset: the behavior on GCE. (B) Cyclic voltammograms for DA 70 μmol L−1 using B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE in 0.05 mol L−1 B-R buffer (pH 2.0) with variation of the scan rate from 30 to 100 mVs−1. (C) The plot of scan rate vs. peak current. (D) The plot of log v vs. log |I|.
(A) Cyclic voltammograms for DA 70 μmol L−1 on B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE in B-R buffer at different pH values (2.0–12.0). (B) Variation of anodic and cathodic peak potentials as a function of pH.

(A) SWV recorded using the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE in the presence of different DA concentrations ranging from 0.7 to 188 μmol L−1. (B) Relationship between the anodic peak current and DA concentration. (C) SW voltammograms were obtained in the 0.7 to 3.48 μmol L−1 concentration range. (D) The corresponding calibration graph for DA.
Schematic diagram of the top-down methods for synthesis of B-GQDs.
Proposed mechanism for the oxidation of DA using the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor.
Comparison between the present work and other composite electrodes for DA determination.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE – porous graphene oxide – Nafion®/GCE; 30rGO/BiVO4/GCE – graphene oxide and bismuth vanadate/GCE; KC-GO/GCE – Kappa-Carrageenan/Graphene Oxide Carbon Magnetite Nanoparticles /GCE; GQDs@MWCNTs/GCE – Graphene quantum dots incorporated with multiwall carbon nanotubes/GCE; GO-GCE – Graphene oxide/GCE; GO-PAN/GCE – Graphene oxide-templated polyaniline microsheets/GCE; 3D RGO/MWNCTs@ZrFeOx/GCE – 3D Reduced graphene oxide /Multiwalled carbon nanotubes@bimetallic-organic gel; ZnO-RGO/GCE – ZnO-decorated reduced graphene oxide (ZnO–RGO) composites; rGO_M/GCE – graphene oxide rGO_M derived; TiN-rGO/GCE – Chrysanthemum-like titanium nitride-reduced graphene oxide (TiN-rGO) composite.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by boron-functionalized N-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) was employed for the electrochemical determination of dopamine (DA), a neurotransmitter. The B-GQDs were obtained by hydrothermal etching of GO with H2O2 and NH3 in the presence of H3BO3. By using various techniques, including transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, molecular absorption spectrometry, fluorescence, ICP-MS, and zeta potential analysis, it was demonstrated that B-GQDs are self-organized in 2D-network, being N-doped GQDs nanoparticles crosslinked by H3BO3. The GCE modification was carried out using the drop-casting method with B-GQDs in Nafion®, previously dispersed through ultrasonication. For the first time, the synthesized nanomaterial was tested in the electroanalysis of neurotransmitters. The B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE modification led to a significant increase in peak currents in the presence of DA. The electrochemical and interfacial characteristics of the sensors were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Under optimized square wave voltammetry and supporting electrolyte conditions, a linear dependence between the anodic peak current and DA concentration was observed in the range of 0.7 to 188 μmol L−1 (R2 = 0.9991). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated at 0.05 μmol L−1. Selectivity was confirmed in the presence of potential interferents found in urine, with interference response below 12 %. The sensor exhibited excellent intra-day and inter-day repeatability, with a relative standard deviation of 4.12 % and 4.66 %, respectively. Electrochemical determination of DA was conducted in artificial urine samples (Surine®), with recovery values of 90–101 %. The new analytical platform B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE demonstrated promising characteristics for application in biological and/or pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of low-cost diagnostic devices with high sensitivity and the ability to provide accurate readouts to users at the point of need has been observed in recent years due to challenges in the medical diagnostics of diseases and abnormal physiological human status. Meanwhile, electrochemical sensors provide required miniaturization and sensitivity, with limited demand for trained personnel and sample preparation [1]. The electrochemical technique is particularly effective in the analytical determination of essential neurotransmitters such as dopamine (DA), which allows for detecting nanomolar and picomolar concentrations. DA, recognized as the pleasure neurotransmitter, plays a fundamental role in the nervous system, transmitting signals from the brain to different organs and body regions [2,3]. The concentration of DA in the body impacts brain function regarding mood, memory, learning, and concentration [3,4]. Therefore, maintaining a healthy DA balance is crucial for physical and psychological well-being. Studies indicate that DA deficiencies are associated with common neurological conditions such as depression, hallucinosis, Alzheimer's, and Parkinson's [5,6]. Accurate detection of DA, especially at deficient concentrations, and developing sensitive and selective detection platforms have become essential in clinical diagnosis.

Among various carbonaceous nanomaterials, graphene-based ones have attracted attention due to their enhanced electrochemical activity, which stems from (1) the large surface area of the 2D graphene sheets, which provide more electroactive sites and increase the sensitivity, and (2) their stability over a wide range of temperatures, which make them reliable conductive materials for electrochemical sensors [7]. Graphene derivatives efficiently stand out for sensing neurotransmitter DA due to their high electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, and strong π-π interactions [8,9]. Recently, advancements in constructing graphene-based sensors for neurotransmitter detection have been driven by the incorporation of various nanomaterials, including metal oxides [10,11], polymeric materials [12–14] and metal nanoparticles [15–17]. Due to their high electrical conductivity and surface-volume ratio, these components enhance the analytical performance of sensors. For example, graphene quantum dots/multiwalled carbon nanotubes composite was used as a membrane for detecting DA release from living cells [18]. Au nanoparticles decorated by polypyrrole/reduced graphene oxide hybrid sheets were proposed for ultrasensitive DA detection [19]. The sensor selectivity can be further increased by incorporating components that can specifically interact with the target into the membrane. For example, incorporating two-dimensional transition-metal carbide layered materials (Ti3C2Tx-MXene) into a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) ensured the electrode sensitivity due to its excellent metallic conductivity, high volumetric capacity, and flexibility. However, only the immobilization of 4-aminophenyl boronic acid made it possible to achieve the necessary selectivity to DA [20]. Similarly, supported on graphene derivatives, pyrene-appended boronic acids provide quantum capacitance-based molecular sensors [21,22]. The utilization of boronic acid derivatives as a component of the electrode membrane is based on the ability of the acid to bind reversibly to diols, a ubiquitous molecular feature in DA [23].

Incorporating boron-containing materials into a selective electrode membrane was further developed by synthesizing supramolecular [24] and nanometric materials [25]. For example, nitrogen and boron-doped reduced graphene oxide was prepared and successfully used to modify a GCE [25]. Several examples of boron-doped graphenic nanomaterials were also obtained recently. Boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) were mainly synthesized from boron carbide [26]. Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanodots were obtained by directly synthesizing nitric acid, boric acid, and urea [27]. As far as we know, boron-containing nanomaterials have not yet been studied as selective membranes of electrochemical sensors for determining DA.

Building upon this approach, the primary objective of this study is to employ the B-GQDs obtained through hydrothermal synthesis from graphene oxide in the presence of H3BO3, resulting in an aqueous suspension of self-organized in 2D-network of boron-functionalized N-doped graphene quantum dots. Various techniques were employed to chemically and structurally characterize the generated nanoparticles. Subsequently, the nanomaterial was dispersed using ultrasonication and applied to the surface of a GCE. For the first time, this work proposes the innovative use of the nanocomposite to create an electrochemical platform, namely B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE. To our knowledge, no studies have been reported exploring B-GQDs in chemically modified electrodes for voltammetric detection of DA.

Potassium permanganate (99 %), hydrogen peroxide (30 %), and ammonia solution (33 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Phosphoric acid (85 %), sulfuric acid (95–98 %), hydrochloric acid (37 %), and sodium hydroxide (97 %) were purchased from Vetec (Brazil), and graphite flakes were purchased from Synth (Brazil). Amicon® stirred cell 50 mL (Sigma-Aldrich) equipped with PTFE filters (0.45 μm) from Sartorius Stedim Biotech (Germany) was used in microfiltration processes. PTFE-hydrophilic 0.22 μm syringe filters from Analítica (Brazil) were utilized before particle characterization or electrode impregnation. Dialysis membranes (CE MWCO 6000–8000) from Spectra/Por® Biotech (USA) were used for particle purification from molecular compounds.

Dopamine hydrochloride (Merck, 98 %), Nafion® (Sigma-Aldrich, 5 %), Surine® Negative Urine Control (Sigma-Aldrich), Ethyl alcohol (Neon, 99.5 %), l-ascorbic acid C6H8O6 (Sigma-Aldrich), Uric acid (Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %), Glucose (Sigma-Aldrich, 96 %), Potassium chloride (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.5 %) were of analytical grade. The buffer solutions used as supporting electrolytes were of three types as follows: McIlvaine made with Na2HPO4 (Vetec, 99 %), and C6H8O7 (Vetec, 99.5 %); Britton-Robinson (B-R) were prepared from boric acid H3BO3 (Vetec, 95 %), acetic acid CH3COOH (Vetec, 95 %), and phosphoric acid H3PO4 (Vetec, 95 %); glycine was prepared from C2H5NO2 (Merck, 99 %) and HCl, at a concentration of 0.1 mol L−1. The solution of potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) and potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) trihydrate (Sigma-Aldrich, >99 %) at 5 mmol L−1 was used as a redox probe for electrochemical measurements. The solutions were prepared using ultrapure water provided by a Milli-Q system (resistivity ca. 18.2 MΩ cm) manufactured by Millipore (Bedford, USA).

Molecular absorption spectrums were measured on UV-M51 from BEL Engineering (Italia) in the 200–850 nm range. SZ-100 nanoparticle analyzer from Horiba (Japan) was used in dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment. For DLS experiments, suspensions were sonicated for 15 min before the measurements, filtrated directly to the cuvette, and kept at 25 °C for 3 min before measurement to equilibrate. The measurements were repeated five times to ensure reproducible results. The particle purification from molecular compounds was performed by dialysis under constant agitation against deionized water for at least 48 h by monitoring the water electroconductivity. Zeta potential measurements were performed on the Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS® 4 mW equipment with a He/Ne laser of wavelength 638 nm, connected to a computer running the Zetasizer software (version 7.12).

MultiMode8 atomic force microscope (AFM) from Bruker (Germany) was utilized in Peak Force Tapping® mode to obtain images of the particle profile. A SCANASYST-AIR probe (Bruker) with a spring constant of 0.4 N/m and 2.0 nm tip-end radius was used for the scanning. The AFM platform was mounted in an environmental chamber, which allows control over the relative humidity and the temperature (10 % and 25 °C). The surface composition of composited was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), using a K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) equipped with a hemispherical electron analyzer and an aluminum anode X-ray source (Kα = 1486.6 eV), providing an energy resolution ∼1 eV. The morphology of the samples was studied using a low-vacuum JSM-6490LV scanning electron microscope (SEM) from JEOL (Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using JEOL JEM 2100F microscope operating at 200 kV. The photoluminescence measurements were performed using RF-6000 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with 1 cm optical path length quartz cuvettes. The concentrations of metal ions were determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) model Nexlon 300× and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP OES) model Optima 7300 DV, both from Perkin Elmer. The pH measurements were carried out using a set with a combined glass electrode from Ohaus (Model ST3100-F) after proper calibration.

Samples of B-GQDs were obtained from graphite flakes in two-step synthesis. Graphene oxide (GO) was first prepared, as it was reported earlier [28]. In brief, 200 mL of the mixture containing H2SO4 (98 %) and H3PO4 (85 %) (9:1 v/v) was slowly added to graphite flakes (1.5 g) in a 500 mL round-bottom flask equipped with a mechanical stir bar. Then solid KMnO4 (9 g) was slowly added under stirring to the graphite suspension in the acids, and then the flask was heated at 50 °C for 12 h. The obtained light-brown mixture was cooled down and slowly poured onto ice (400 mL) containing 30 % H2O2 (20 mL), giving an orange suspension. The suspension was sonicated for 30 min at room temperature, and the resulting GO was separated by centrifugation. The precipitate was resuspended in 2 % HCl under sonification and then filtrated. The procedure was repeated several times until the negative result on Mn impurity in the solution was confirmed by ICP OES. Finally, the aqueous suspension of GO was purified from water-soluble components by dialysis (48 h, membrane 8000–10,000 Da), concentrated on Amicon stirred cell using 0.45 μm PTFE filter, washed with ethanol (3 times) and ether, and dried in vacuum, giving 1.3 g (87 %) of fine semi-transparent dark-brown flakes.

In the second step, the GO (60 mg) was dispersed in deionized water (20 mL) with 1.5 mL of ammonia solution (25 %), 3.0 mL of H2O2 (30 % solution) and 100 mg of H3BO3. The sample was treated at 180 °C for 8 h in a closed Teflon cylinder reactor under hydrothermal conditions, then cooled naturally and centrifuged at 15000 rpm (37,000 g) for 10 min, collecting brown color supernatant containing a suspension of B-GQDs. Further, 0.9 % NaCl was added to the supernatant, and coagulated B-GQDs were separated by centrifugation and then redispersed in water. Finally, B-GQDs were purified from NaCl and other molecular impurities by dialysis (48 h, membrane 8000–10,000 Da) and kept in the form of suspension in water at 4 °C. Chemical analysis of B-GQDs by ICP-MS confirms the presence of B element with a concentration of about 116 ppm.

The PalmSens2 potentiostat (Palm Instruments BV – Netherlands) and PSTrace software (version 5.9) were used for the electrochemical studies. The setup containing three electrodes, such as GCE as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl/KCl(sat.) as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode, were used for electrochemical analyses. All the measurements were carried out in the electrolytic cell with a volume of 10 mL at room temperature.

The sensors were characterized using a 5 mmol L−1 solution of potassium ferricyanide [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− as the probe, with a 0.1 mol L−1 KCl solution, over a potential range from −0.2 to +0.8 V (vs. Ag/AgCl/KCl(sat.)). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was applied in the same potential range, followed by a reverse sweep from +0.8 to −0.2 V using a scan rate (ν) of 50 mV s−1. The pH study was set at pH values from 2.0 to 12.0, and the effect of the scan rate was carried out from 30 to 100 mV s−1 to evaluate electrochemical processes and mechanisms.

The determination of DA was performed using square wave voltammetry (SWV) under the interval parameters of frequency (ƒ: 10–100 Hz), pulse amplitude (a: 10–100 mV), and potential increment (ΔEs: 1–10 mV). Finally, the values of ƒ = 50 Hz, a = 50 mV, and ΔEs = 5 mV were chosen to construct the calibration curve with successive additions of aliquots of DA.

The GCE surface was carefully polished with alumina (0.3 and 0.05 μm), washed with distilled water, and sonicated with an ethanol/water solution for 15 min. For the electrode modification, three solutions were prepared: Nafion®, B-GQDs, and B-GQDs/Nafion®, each with a volume of 1000 μL. The B-GQDs and B-GQDs/Nafion® solutions were dispersed by ultrasonic for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the GCE was modified with 5 μL of Nafion® solution (0.15 %) using the drop-coating method and then taken to the oven at 60 °C for 5 min for complete solvent evaporation. The same procedure was repeated for each modification.

A new step in this process is hydrothermal etching of GO into GQDs in water solution of ammonia and H2O2 in the presence of H3BO3. Hydrothermal etching by H2O2 in the presence of ammonia was selected because it allows the preparation of N-doped GQDs from a few-layer GO with high yield [29]. The introduction of H3BO3 to this procedure allows obtaining a self-organized 2D-network, where N-GQDs are cross-linked by boric acid, Scheme 1 .

Hydrothermal synthesis of B-GQDs performed at the same experimental conditions as N-GQDs synthesis, but in the presence of H3BO3, resulted in the preparation of water suspension of B-GQDs, which can contain a small amount of precursor GO that can be easily separated from the target products by centrifugation.

On the contrary to water suspension of N-GQDs, the molecular absorption spectra of B-GQDs demonstrate a broad absorption shoulder in the visible part of the spectrum, corresponding to π-plasmonic interaction in the graphene core of the particles. At the same time, n −
π ∗
transitions from carbonyl groups (at the wavelength of ~280 nm) is less intensive for B-GQDs than for N-GQDs, indicating a reduction of oxygen-containing fragments located on the particle borders (Scheme 1) [30]. Also, blue-shift for π −
π ∗
transition band is observed for B-GQDs (Fig. S1A). Unlike N-GQDs, the network of boron-functionalized N-GQDs does not exhibit noticeable photoluminescence indicating strong interaction between graphenic cores for the particles. This observation correlates with the presence of a π-plasmonic band in the molecular absorption spectra of B-GQDs. The peculiarities observed in the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of B-GQDs indicate the aggregation of nanoparticles into a network [31], presumably due to their binding with boric acid, as it is lustrated in Scheme 1.

The TEM image of B-GQDs confirms the formation of a porous 2D-network of cross-linked nanoparticles (Fig. 1A – D). The distance observed between fringes in the HRTEM image of B-GQDs is about 0.36 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane (inset Fig. 1D). By extreme dilution and ultrasonic treatment, it was able to obtain and study small fragments of B-GQDs by AFM, which shows that B-GQDs fragments have a porous structure with about 5.5 nm height (Fig. 1E and F).

The DLS study of as-prepared B-GQDs demonstrates that the suspension has polymodal particle distribution with two populations 40 ± 10 nm and 500 ± 140 nm. But after the purification procedure was applied (by coagulation of B-GQDs in NaCl with further re-distribution on water), the population of the smaller particles essentially decreased (Fig. 1G, samples 2,3), which confirms the separation of B-GQDs and N-GQDs by the procedure selected.

Finally, investigation of the Z-potential for B-GQDs revealed that the absolute value of zeta potential for B-GQDs at pH = 6.8 is about −36.4 mV, smaller than for GQDs and N-GQDs [32], which confirms the proposed scheme of 2D-network formation. Also, the value of zeta potential is high enough to ensure the stability of B-GQDs suspension in water due to interparticle electrostatic repulsion.

XPS measurements were performed to determine the elemental composition of the B-GQDs and their chemical state. The XPS survey spectra of B-GQDs and pristine GO showed dominant peaks at 288.8 eV and 531.5 eV that can be assigned to C1s, and O1s binding energy, respectively.

Samples of B-GQDs also demonstrate the presence of N1s (8.8 %) at 399.5 eV, Na1s (2 %) at 1066 eV, and B1s (about 1 %) at 192 eV (Fig. 2A). It is good to mention that the C/O atom ratio for B-GQDs is about 3.0, which is much higher than that of GO (1.9–1.8) and N-GQDs (1.7) [21]. This indicates that B-GQDs are less oxygenated than GO and N-GQDs, approaching the atomic ratio C/O for rGO (6.1) [33]. This fact is crucial to understand the electrochemical properties of B-GQDs. The presence of Na1s peak in the XPS spectrum of B-GQDs can be attributed to residual NaCl in the sample.

For this study, it is essential to note that the geometry and position of C1s peak for GO and B-GQDs differ significantly (Fig. 2B). High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s peak for GO can be deconvoluted into three principal components, corresponding to binding energy in C–C/C=C (285.0 eV), CO (287.0 eV), and O–C=O (289.0 eV) fragments (Fig. 2B). Hydrothermal treatment of GO in the presence of H3BO3 remarkably decreased the intensity of carbon‑oxygen fragments and the peak corresponded to C1s binding energy in C–C/C=C bonds (284.9 eV) became dominant. Besides, B-GQDs contain about 18.8 % of C–O/C–N (286.5 eV) and 7.7 % of O–C=O (288.6 eV) fragments, which is also common for N-GQDs [21]. However, the essential difference is observed for N-GQDs and B-GQDs in the atomic composition of CO (287.8 eV) fragments. For N-GQDs, it is about three times higher [21] than for B-GQDs (8.4 %) - Fig. 2B. This observation is in good agreement with the results obtained from the molecular absorption spectra, indicating partial reduction of carbonyl fragments located on the particle borders. The difference in nitrogen chemical state for N-GQDs and B-GQDs is less pronounced. From high-resolution N1s XPS spectra, it can be concluded that among all nitrogen-containing moieties of B-GQDs, 39 % belongs to N-5 fragments, with binding energy at 399.9 eV, 56 % to pyridine-like fragments with binding energy at 400.3 eV and 5 % to N-Q fragments with binding energy at 402.7 eV (Fig. 2D). This observation confirms formation of 2D-network from N-GQDs. High-resolution B1s spectra of B-GQDs indicate the presence of B-O/B-C fragments confirming the proposed scheme of 2D-network formation from N-GQDs through their crosslinking with H3BO3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of zeta potential to evaluate the charge stability of Nafion®, B-GQDs, and B-GQDs/Nafion®
ce:section.ce:para: Nafion® was a dispersant and film-forming agent to prevent the detachment of B-GQDs from the surface of the GCE. Additionally, the hydrophilic negatively charged sulfonate groups in the Nafion® polymer structure contributed to the accumulation of positively charged molecules through electrostatic interactions, resulting in enhanced sensitivity in the measurements [34]. In previous studies, Nafion® was successfully utilized in a mixture with GO to detect positive charges [35].

In the case of the Nafion® solution, the dissociation of sulfonic acid end-groups on side chains led to negatively charged molecules, as indicated by a zeta potential of −18 mV for the Nafion dispersion with a concentration of 0.15 % (Fig. S2A). The significant difference in zeta potential between B-GQDs (Fig. S2B) and B-GQDs/Nafion® (Fig. S2C) is attributed to the adsorption of Nafion® molecules on the surfaces of B-GQDs, resulting in a higher negative charge density (in the side chains of Nafion®) [36]. Consequently, a higher zeta potential was observed. A higher zeta potential was noted in the self-assembly of N-GQDs into a porous structure with pore sizes below 100 nm. Therefore, the Nafion® sample exhibited low stability, while the B-GQDs solution demonstrated moderate stability, and the B-GQDs/Nafion® combination showed high stability, suggesting an interaction between Nafion® and B-GQDs. Based on these results, the B-GQDs/Nafion® modifier was chosen to continue the studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of GCE and B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of the sensor was evaluated in potassium ferricyanide [Fe(CN)6]3−/4– test solution, as described in section 2.4 of this study. Fig. S3A displays the CV curves for B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE and demonstrates a notable reduction in both cathodic and anodic peak currents compared to the GCE.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also applied to investigate the electrode and the sensor's interfacial charge transfer resistance (Rct). Nyquist plots and the equivalent circuit of Randles are presented in Fig. S3B. The equivalent circuit is shown in Fig. S3C and D. The R1 component is related to the solution resistance, R2 may be associated with the charge transfer resistance, C1 is defined as the double-layer capacitance, and W1 is the Warburg constant related to diffusion contribution. For GCE, EIS measurements showed a semicircle profile with linear behavior at lower frequencies, indicating the diffusional process of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4–. However, for B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE, this behavior was not observed, suggesting that the process may be adsorptive. The Rct values were estimated as the semicircle diameter and were found to be 2.15 and 8.68 KΩ for GCE and B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE, respectively. The reduction in peak intensity and the increase in charge transfer resistance may be attributed to the high surface charge of B-GQDs/Nafion®, as indicated by zeta potential measurements. Due to this high surface charge, ferricyanide ions are repelled at the electrode surface, thus restricting electrochemical reactions for the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4– solution [37].

The electrochemical behavior of different modified electrodes in the presence of DA in a 0.05 mol L−1 B-R solution (pH 2.0) was investigated using the CV technique, with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Fig. 3A illustrates the responses obtained from CV curves for GCE, B-GQDs/GCE, Nafion®/GCE, and B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE using a concentration of 70 μmol L−1 of DA. In the case of the GCE, a well-defined redox peak was observed, with anodic and cathodic peaks occurring at +0.73 V and +0.26 V, respectively (inserted Fig. 3A).

Upon introducing the B-GQDs modification to the GCE electrode, an increase in the intensities of both anodic and cathodic currents was noticed. Furthermore, the potentials of the anodic and cathodic peaks exhibited a negative shift (Fig. 3A, red line). The electrode modified with Nafion®/GCE demonstrated improved electrochemical performance, particularly in the oxidation and reduction of DA (Fig. 3A, blue line). In the case of the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor, the potentials of the anodic and cathodic peaks were negatively shifted to +0.50 V and + 0.41 V, respectively (Fig. 3A, green line).

During the optimization step, the electrochemical properties of B-GQDs and N-GQDs were evaluated for dopamine (70 μmol L−1). The results for B-GQDs demonstrated a high current signal for DA when compared to N-GQDs (Fig. S4). This enhanced performance might be attributed to the ability of boric acid to bind to diols, thereby increasing the affinity of B-GQDs for DA.

The B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor stood out compared to the GCE electrode and other analyzed sensors, presenting a significantly higher current response. Additionally, it induced a shift in the oxidation and reduction peaks to lower potentials, indicating electrocatalytic properties for DA oxidation and reduction reactions. This improvement can be attributed to the interaction between B-GQDs and Nafion®, forming an ideal coadjutant film. This film acts as an efficient electron promoter, enhancing the electrocatalytic reactions of DA oxidation and reduction [38]. Fueled by these promising observations, new studies have been conducted using the electrochemical sensor B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE.

The analysis of the variation of the scan rate is crucial to understanding essential aspects of the reaction mechanism and sensor operation, as well as to determine the optimal reading condition for DA determination. Fig. 3B shows the cyclic voltammograms for a concentration of 70 μmol L−1 of DA in 0.05 mol L−1 B-R (pH 2.0), using the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor, with the scan rate varying between 30 and 100 mVs−1.

In a quasi-reversible electrochemical reaction, the current is influenced by both mass transfer and charge transfer. In these cases, the Nernst equation is approximately valid. Generally, these systems may exhibit reversibility at low and irreversibility at higher scan rates. Therefore, the quasi-reversible process is observed at intermediate scan rates [39,40]. A common characteristic in quasi-reversible systems is the increasing separation between Epa and Epc as the scan rate increases. In other words, the value of ∆Ep varies as the scan rate increases [39].

According to the Randles- Ševčík equation, when the peak current varies linearly with the square root of the scan rate, a diffusional process occurs between the analyte and the electrode surface; for processes that are controlled by adsorption, a linear variation of peak current versus scan rate is observed [39,40]. In Fig. 3C, a linear relationship between the anodic and cathodic peak currents and the scan rate was observed, which can be expressed as Ipa (μA) = 565.04v + 0.09 and Ipc (μA) = −303.85 v – 3.64. The correlation coefficients were 0.9975 and 0.9963 for Ipa and Ipc, respectively. These results suggest that the process is controlled by adsorption.

A correlation between the double logarithm of the anodic and cathodic peak currents and the scan rate was performed to validate the results obtained previously and clarify any doubts about the type of process occurring on the surface of the proposed sensor. Shih et al. (2004) proposed a scale to correlate the slope of the log I versus log v graph and determine if the process is diffusional, adsorptive, or mixed. According to the scale, a slope equal to or <0.5 indicates a diffusional process. A slope equal to or >1.0 suggests an adsorptive process, and when the slope is between 0.5 and 1.0, it means the occurrence of mixed processes, controlled by both diffusion and adsorption [41].

Fig. 3D shows the linear dependence between log Ipa and Ipc versus log v, which resulted in slopes of 1.02 and 0.84 for DA oxidation and reduction, respectively. It was observed that the slope for the DA oxidation process was close to the theoretical value of 1.0, suggesting that the adsorptive process controls DA oxidation. On the other hand, the reduction process presented a slope of 0.84, indicating that the reduction is controlled by a mixed process [42]. Therefore, it can be suggested that the adsorptive process predominantly controls the electrochemical response at the sensor interface for DA oxidation, while DA reduction is predominantly controlled by the mixed process.

To determine the anodic and cathodic electron transfer coefficient, α, the peak potentials of the redox peaks were plotted against the logarithmic scan rate. A linear correlation was observed between these two parameters when log v was at 30 mVs−1, due to a controlled adsorption process (Fig. S5). Based on the anodic and cathodic peak potentials (Epa and Epc) and using Laviron theory for anodic and cathodic processes, the following equation can be established:
(1)
m =
2,303 RT
1 − α
nF
where (n) is the number of electrons transferred, R is the ideal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), T is the temperature (298 K), F is the Faraday constant (96.485C mol−1), α is the electron transfer coefficient, and (m) is the slope. It was predicted that ideally, for an irreversible process, α would be equal to 0.5 [43–45]. By assigning this value to α, it is possible to determine the number of mols of electrons transferred between DA and the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor. The determined values for the number of mols of electrons in the oxidation and reduction of DA were 1.08 and 1.17, respectively, close to 1. These results agree with those obtained in the previous pH study, where the involvement of the same number of mols of electrons and protons was highlighted.

Applying the theoretical electron transfer coefficient (α) made it possible to determine the number of electrons involved in the reaction. With this value, it is possible to assess αa, which is the calculated value of the electron transfer coefficient. By rewriting Eq. (1) and applying the values described above, the following values of αa were found for oxidation and reduction: 0.49 and 0.41, respectively. It can be observed that the found value was very close to the predicted value, indicating that the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor has a quasi-reversible electronic process characteristic [46,47], a fact that corroborates with the results observed in Fig. 3B.

Therefore, it can be concluded that the oxidation of DA on the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor presents an adsorption-controlled process and involves the proportional participation of 1 mol of electrons to 1 mol of protons. The proposed mechanism in Scheme 2 agrees with previous reports [38,48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and supporting electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: The CV study was performed at different pH values ranging from 2.0 to 12.0, as illustrated in Fig. 4A. Peak potentials exhibited pH dependence, changing to negative potentials with increasing pH within the studied range. This can be attributed to the influence of the proton on the oxidation and reduction of DA. The most sensitive range for DA is shown in Fig. 4A. From the CV scans in Fig. 4A, a linear relationship between anodic and cathodic peak potentials versus pH was identified, with slopes of 0.052 and 0.055 pH mV−1 (Fig. 4B). The results corresponded closely to theoretical Nernst values, indicating a similar number of moles of protons and electrons involved in the redox reaction. These findings align with literature reports on oxidation and reduction in the pH range evaluated, indicating 1 proton and 1 electron in the redox mechanism [38,48]. Furthermore, the highest current peak was obtained in an acidic medium (pH 2.0); this value was chosen for subsequent optimization studies.

Initially, the influence of the supporting electrolyte was evaluated by varying the type of buffer solution at a concentration of 0.1 mol L−1 containing 70 μmol L−1 of DA, considering that proton transport and the current signal can be affected in the presence of different buffers species [40,49,50]. Fig. S6A and C show that the most intense current peak was obtained in the presence of B-R buffer at a concentration of 0.05 mol L−1, which is why it was selected as the supporting.

Comparative studies of three distinct voltammetric techniques were carried out to determine the best option for the oxidation of DA. The evaluated techniques were linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV). To ensure a fair comparison, the parameters of each technique were adjusted to maintain the scan rate during the measurements at 50 mVs−1.

Fig. S7 shows the voltammograms obtained from the three proposed techniques. The results showed that LSV presented the slightest signal regarding the analyte. A more intense analytical signal was obtained when using DPV. However, SWV generated the highest current signal, which was expected since the reaction is quasi-reversible [39], as described in section 3.5 of this work.

Thus, it was decided to use the SWV technique in the application and determination studies. The parameters were optimized to obtain an ideal balance between the resulting current intensity and peak resolution. The experimental parameters of SWV were varied within the following ranges: pulse application frequency (f: 10–100 Hz), pulse amplitude (a: 10–100 mV), and potential increment (ΔEs: 1–10 mV). Fig. S8A-F shows the voltammograms and the dependencies of the resulting peak current as a function of the experimental parameters of the technique. At the end of the technique optimization study, the best parameters for further studies were determined and are described in section 2.4 of this work.

SWV carried out the electrochemical determination of DA in a potential range of −0.2 to +0.9 V (vs. Ag/AgCl(sat.)) using the optimized experimental conditions. Fig. 5A shows the SW voltammograms for different concentrations of DA. To investigate the linear range, well-defined peaks can be observed at a potential of +0.585 V, whose current intensity increases proportionally with the added DA concentration (0.7 to 188 μmol L−1). In Fig. 5B, the linear dependence of the anodic peak current with DA concentration can be observed. This result reveals the existence of three different linear ranges, suggesting an initial saturation of the electrode surface at concentrations above 3.48 μmol L−1. This saturation can be attributed to the possible adsorption of species on the sensor surface, which reduces the number of available redox sites for electrochemical processes.

Fig. 5C shows the calibration curve graph obtained for the lowest concentrations of DA, ranging from 0.7 to 3.48 μmol L−1. These results were recorded to obtain a calibration curve in the highest sensitivity range for DA analysis. The calibration curve was obtained in triplicate, and the linear relationship can be expressed as I
SW /μA = 3.99 (±0.05) -1.62 (±0.07) [DA] (μmol L−1), with an R2 of 0.9991 (Fig. 5D). The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were calculated using the following equations:
(2)
LOD =
3 , 3 σ
S
(3)
LOQ =
10 σ
S
where S is the slope and σ is the standard deviation of the mean value for 10 blank voltammograms, determined according to the recommendations of the IUPAC [51]. The LOD and LOQ values found were 0.05 μmol L−1 and 0.18 μmol L−1, respectively. To establish the uniqueness of the developed sensor, we compared the LOD and electrochemical performance with previously reported sensors. This comparison is presented in Table 1 . The results presented in this work demonstrate that the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor presented sensitivity that, in some cases, is superior to the sensors mentioned in the literature. This analysis reinforces the confidence that the proposed B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor is a viable option for DA detection.

The selectivity of the present sensor was evaluated in the presence of 7 μmol L−1 DA and 70 μmol L−1 of possible interferents found in urine. The interference study was performed under optimized conditions without pre-treatment steps. SWV measurements were separately performed for each interferent to ensure the appropriate interference response of the tested compounds. The results of the relative interference are presented in Fig. S9A-D, as follows: DA without interferent; (AA) ascorbic acid; (UA) uric acid; and (GLY) glucose. The relative response was estimated considering the DA voltammograms in the presence of each potential interfering compound. The interference was <12 % for all tested compounds indicating the sensor's high selectivity [61]. The high selectivity can be explained by the electrocatalytic effect of B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE, plus the impact of pH that shifted the oxidation potential of DA to a more positive zone. This fact was observed in the pH study, and thus, the possible interferents found in urine did not affect the DA oxidation potential.

Repeatability, specifically intra- and inter-day precision, was investigated in the presence of 70 μmol L−1 of the analyte. Before each measurement, a new, complete modification of the GCE was constructed. The peak intensities corresponding to each voltammogram are shown in Fig. S9E. To evaluate intra-day precision, five different electrodes were prepared on the same day and used to quantify the same level of analyte concentration. Inter-day precision was assessed using the same procedure with five modified electrodes prepared on subsequent days. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values were estimated based on the analyte's anodic peak current and were 4.12 % and 4.66 % for intra-day and inter-day repeatability, respectively [61].

The stability of the sensor was assessed using the cyclic CV technique to investigate the behavior of DA in response to multiple cycles. After performing 50 voltammetric measurements (as shown in Fig. S10A), it was observed that the oxidation and reduction peaks of DA did not remain constant from the first to 29 cycles (Fig. S10B). This was due to the variation in intensity during the scans, where the intensity of the first scan was the lowest but increased with each subsequent cycle. In the following cycles, from the 29th to the 50th, a decrease in the anodic and cathodic current intensities was observed, although these reductions were insignificant (Fig. S10C). The standard deviation of the 50 consecutive cycles was 3.39 % and 4.24 % for the anodic and cathodic peaks, respectively. After 50 cycles, the electrochemical response reached 116.2 % for anodic peaks and 120.3 % for cathodic peaks of the original peak value. These results indicate that the modified electrode can be successfully applied in repeated experiments every fifty cycles, maintaining adequate analytical performance [61].
The recovery procedure was applied to synthetic urine samples to validate the proposed method. These samples were fortified with concentrations of 0.8, 0.9, and 1.0 μmol L−1 of DA (n = 3) (Table 2 ).

The concentrations were calculated using the standard addition method, and the percentage recovery values were determined by the ratio between the found value and the added value multiplied by 100. The recovery data in Table 2 reflect the method's accuracy as the average DA recoveries varied satisfactorily between 90 % and 101 % [61]. These results indicate that the B-GQDs/Nafion®/GCE sensor can be effectively applied for DA determination with good selectivity and stability.

Nanoparticles of B-GQDs were synthesized and characterized using different techniques. For the first time, the nanomaterial was applied in the field of electroanalysis. The proposed sensor was prepared by drop-casting B-GQDs/Nafion® on a GCE, exhibiting an excellent electrocatalytic response. Using SWV, achieving excellent selectivity and a low detection limit for DA quantification was possible. The low RSD values obtained from intra and inter-day repeatability indicate promising characteristics for routine analyses. The analytical method was successfully applied to detect DA in synthetic urine samples. All results suggest that the GCE modified with B-GQDs/Nafion® is a promising sensor for the electrochemical detection of DA in real samples.
